title: String basics
instruction:
Strings are immutable sequences of characters in double quotes.

Run this.
successMessage: Length gives the character count. ToUpper() returns a new string — the original is unchanged.

title: String interpolation
instruction:
`$"text {expression}"` interpolates values directly into the string.

Run this.
successMessage: Any C# expression can go inside {}. Interpolated strings are evaluated at runtime.

title: String methods
instruction:
C# strings have many useful methods.

Run this.
successMessage: Trim removes whitespace. Replace swaps text. Contains and StartsWith return bool. IndexOf returns the position of a character.

title: Substring and slicing
instruction:
`Substring(start, length)` extracts part of a string.

Run this.
successMessage: Substring(start, length) is classic C#. s[0..5] and s[^6..] are modern C# 8 range syntax. ^ counts from the end.

title: Split and Join
instruction:
`Split` breaks a string into an array. `String.Join` combines an array.

Run this.
successMessage: Split returns an array of strings. String.Join glues them back together with a separator.

title: String comparison
instruction:
`==` compares string values. `string.Compare` gives ordering.

Run this.
successMessage: == is case-sensitive. OrdinalIgnoreCase makes it case-insensitive. Compare returns -1 (less), 0 (equal), or 1 (greater).

title: Format strings
instruction:
`string.Format` with format specifiers controls number formatting.

Run this.
successMessage: Format specifiers after : control presentation. F2=fixed 2 decimals, C=currency, N0=number with commas, P=percent.

title: StringBuilder
instruction:
`StringBuilder` is efficient for building strings in loops.

Run this.
successMessage: StringBuilder avoids creating a new string for every concatenation. For 5 items it barely matters; for 5,000 it is crucial.
